channel.title: NYT > Style
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: These Designers Want to Dress Your Bed
item.description: Millaux sheets are made to order. But the designers don’t want the pillowcases to match. Ever.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Iredale
item.media:credit: Yael Malka for The New York Times

item.title: Alex Cooper, Host of ‘Call Her Daddy,’ Is Pregnant With First Child
item.description: Alex Cooper, the host of the popular podcast “Call Her Daddy,” announced in an Instagram post that she was expecting her first child. The news shook the internet.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Evan Agostini/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Alex Cooper, left, and Matt Kaplan.

item.title: Comparing ‘The Devil Wears Prada,’ Then and Now
item.description: The “before” and “after” versions of the cast are just the beginning.
item.dc:creator: Rhonda Garelick
item.media:credit: Top: Stuart Ramson/Associated Press; Bottom: Kate Green/Getty Images
item.media:description: The cast promoting “The Devil Wears Prada,” above, and nearly the same group promoting the sequel 20 years later.

item.title: Frida Kahlo Photos: What Made the Painter a ‘Living Work of Art’
item.description: Plaster corsets and Tehuana skirts, men’s wear and spider monkeys: These are the things that made Frida Frida.
item.dc:creator: Louis Lucero II
item.category: El Ultimo Sueno de Frida y Diego (Opera)
item.media:credit: Getty Images; Ricky Rhodes for The New York Times

item.title: Gucci Got Tom Brady and Cindy Crawford to Strut Their Stuff in Times Square
item.description: And other memorable moments, and clothes, from Demna’s first cruise show.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: Why Milk Is the Minimalist Fragrance of the Moment
item.description: Glossier, Mugler and the Nue Co. are just a handful of brands selling milk-scented fragrances.
item.dc:creator: Gina Cherelus

item.title: What Is a Bookstore Without Books?
item.description: Booksellers are trying to figure out how to market audiobooks in person. At a new pop-up, Audible is betting on listening rooms, branded merch and “story tenders.”
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi and Seth Caplan
item.media:description: Audible, the audiobook giant owned by Amazon, staged a pop-up bookstore — without any physical books — in an effort to market audiobooks IRL.

item.title: In Italy, Princess Catherine Puts Her Wardrobe to Work
item.description: Suits her, sir.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Phil Noble
item.media:description: Princess Catherine in a pantsuit by the London designer Edeline Lee.

item.title: Tickle Me Elmo and the ‘Elmo-Mania’ That Consumed America
item.description: In 1996, the giggling stuffed doll turned holiday shopping into chaos, a preview of every sneaker drop, PlayStation launch and Taylor Swift ticket rush to come.
item.dc:creator: Amisha Padnani
item.media:credit: Steve Hart for The New York Times
item.media:description: There was a mad rush to grab one of the few remaining Tickle Me Elmo dolls at a Toys “R” Us store in Manhattan on Dec. 4, 1996.

item.title: At RuPaul’s ‘Stop! That! Train!’ Premiere, Drag Queens and Gay Icons Served ‘Choo-Choo Realness’
item.description: In Los Angeles, at the premiere of “Stop! That! Train!” a disaster film parody set on the runaway Glamazonian Express, the dress code was “choo-choo realness.”
item.dc:creator: Juno Carmel
item.media:description: RuPaul, center, at the hot pink carpet premiere of “Stop! That! Train!” with alumni of “RuPaul’s Drag Race.”

item.title: Everlane Represented a Millennial Ideal. Is It Dead?
item.description: The ‘radically transparent’ company is reportedly being bought by Shein, a fast fashion behemoth.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Justin Kaneps for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Everlane sweater, seen here in better days for the brand.

item.title: In Musk vs. OpenAI, the Real Winner Was the Suit
item.description: It was the one thing on which every major player could agree.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for the New York Times, Josh Edelson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: How Has Your Shopping Changed in the Era of Weight-Loss Drugs?
item.description: In the last few years, weight-loss drugs like Ozempic have altered how many Americans think about clothes and getting dressed.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The popularity of weight-loss drugs has affected many facets of American life, including how people shop and dress.

item.title: Street Style Look of the Week: A Couple With a Shared Passion for Fringe
item.description: They found someone who will always say yes to a second (or fourth) belt.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha

item.title: Cannes Film Festival 2026: All the Red Carpet Photos
item.description: Photos from the South of France, where Ruth Negga, Sharon Stone, John Travolta and more went all out for the first week of the annual film festival.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Sameer Al-Doumy/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Sharon Stone in Miss Sohee for the screening of “Fjord” at the Cannes Film Festival.

item.title: My Boss Is an Alcoholic. What Should I Do?
item.description: Plus, a bona fide “kids say the darnedest things” moment at a company party.
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are So Many Athletes So Blinged Out?
item.description: A reader wonders how major jewelry has become a part of competition gear.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Hollie Adams/Reuters
item.media:description: Aryna Sabalenka on the podium at the Australian Open in January, now the face of the Material Good jewelry line.

item.title: Shoppers’ Frenzy for ‘Royal Pop’ Pocket Watches Forces Swatch to Shut Stores
item.description: The pocket watches, a collaboration with the luxury timepiece maker Audemars Piguet, drew crowds to stores and malls around the world.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Salvatore Di Nolfi
item.media:description: Customers had been waiting outside a Swatch store in Geneva. The company closed dozens of its stores on Saturday as crowds lined up to snag its new pocket watch, a collaboration with Audemars Piguet.

item.title: Oysters, Champagne and Billionaires Buying Art
item.description: When the European Fine Art Foundation alights in Manhattan, it’s something like a billionaire version of the classic television game show “Supermarket Sweep.”
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay

item.title: Brides Are No Longer Tossing the Bouquet. They’re Keeping It.
item.description: Some newlyweds, rethinking an old custom that puts a premium on marriage, are making more sustainable arrangements.
item.dc:creator: Maria Santa Poggi

item.title: The Audemars Piguet and Swatch Collab That Broke the Internet
item.description: A collaboration between Audemars Piguet and Swatch sent watch fans into a frenzy. But for some, it was a letdown.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art

item.title: Tenderly Tracking My Husband
item.description: As I watched my husband’s dot go to places he loved, I felt newly connected to him. But could I protect him?
item.dc:creator: Caroline Bailey
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: A Kiss and a Proposal — All on Their First Date
item.description: Dr. John Cook and Sylvia Auton had known each other socially for 15 years through her husband. After their spouses passed, poetry and an accidental FaceTime changed their trajectory.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sylvia Auton and John Cook were married at St. James’ Episcopal Church, Leesburg, Va., on May 9.

item.title: They Found Love On and Off the Tennis Court
item.description: Jeffrey Petigrow and Emma Famili met in 2022 at a clinic that helps children with autism build skills through tennis.
item.dc:creator: Kaitlin Menza

item.title: How Two Bookworms Wrote Their Own Love Story
item.description: Stefanie Joseph and Christopher Richards, who met on a dating app three years ago, immediately bonded over their love of reading.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora

item.title: Frank Stack, Painter Who Secretly Drew ‘The Adventures of Jesus,’ Dies at 88
item.description: For 20 years, he hid his identity behind the nom de plume Foolbert Sturgeon as he chronicled Christ’s encounters with modern-day hypocrites in comic-book form.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Joe Ando Has Dressed Celebrities. Now He Wants to Dress You.
item.description: Joe Ando-Hirsh made a name for himself with bespoke gowns. The ambition of his new brand is to dress the average woman.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Reggie Mccafferty for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joe Ando-Hirsh is not your typical designer. His career began on TikTok, from his parents’ garage.

item.title: The Family Branding of Sean Duffy’s Road Trip Reality Show
item.description: A trailer heralds the latest spinoff from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.category: The Great American Road Trip (TV Program)

item.title: UCF Commencement Speaker Draws Boos After A.I. Remarks
item.description: Humanities students made their displeasure known at the University of Central Florida.
item.dc:creator: Gabriella Gershenson

item.title: When Couples and Their Parents Can’t Agree on Wedding Details
item.description: Boomer and Gen X parents helping to pay for a wedding are sometimes finding themselves at odds with the couple over what qualifies as a must-have.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce

item.title: Niko Rubio Is a Woman on the Verge of a Nervy Breakthrough
item.description: Niko Rubio, a bilingual songwriter from Los Angeles who has collaborated with the No Doubt lead singer, is having a major breakthrough.
item.dc:creator: Lizzy Goodman and Ariel Fisher
item.media:credit: Ariel Fisher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Niko Rubio imagines “Sunday Girl’s” titular character as a nun leading a double life: By day, she fulfills her duties at the convent; by night, she performs as a sultry lounge singer.

item.title: Rashidah Ismaili Has Hosted the Legendary Salon d’Afrique in Her Harlem Home for Decades
item.description: The writer Rashidah Ismaili honors a storied literary tradition by hosting Salon d’Afrique, a creative gathering for international Black luminaries, in her Harlem home.
item.dc:creator: Aida Alami and Naima Green
item.media:description: Rashidah Ismaili has brought together international artists to share meals and discuss their work in her Harlem apartment, where she’s resided since 1984.

item.title: The Good List: Voice Memos and Snail Mail
item.description: Plus, your own private screening room.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Tiny Love Stories: ‘Life Suddenly Made Sense’
item.description: Modern Love in miniature, featuring reader-submitted stories of no more than 100 words.
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: As a Sober Person, How Should I Serve Alcohol to Friends at Dinner Parties?
item.description: A reader struggles with how to reconcile his newfound sobriety with his desire to be a gracious host when inviting friends into his home.
item.dc:creator: Philip Galanes
item.media:credit: Miguel Porlan

item.title: Want to See the Epstein Files in Print? Here Are the 3,437 Volumes.
item.description: Documents related to Jeffrey Epstein, taking up more than 3,400 volumes and weighing over eight tons, have been printed and bound for display in a TriBeCa gallery.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gallery at 101 Reade Street, in Tribeca, now houses bookshelves filled with millions of law enforcement documents related to the investigation of Jeffrey Epstein, printed and bound, which take up 3,437 volumes.

item.title: Eating Healthy? No, They’re Eating Biblically.
item.description: A diet inspired by the Bible has found new audiences online in the Make America Healthy Again era.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher

item.title: How Our Personal Stories Became the New Soap Opera
item.description: Even though only four original soap operas remain on the air, our appetite for high drama has never been more insatiable.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Druckman
item.category: The Bold and the Beautiful (TV Program)
The Young and the Restless (TV Program)
item.media:credit: NBC, via Getty Images
